author: Sofia Martínez
title: The Service Business Marketing Funnel That Actually Converts Leads
description: Build a predictable client acquisition engine for your service business. We show you the exact funnel stages that convert 15-25% of prospects into paying clients.

sections.p: Most service businesses don't have a funnel—they have a prayer. You get a few referrals, land a big job, then panic for 60 days waiting for the next lead. We've worked with HVAC companies, plumbing contractors, and pest control businesses that went from feast-and-famine to 8-12 qualified leads per week by building a real funnel. The difference: they stopped treating every prospect the same and started moving them through distinct stages with specific messaging and offers at each step.

sections.h2: Stage 1: Awareness—Get Found Before You're Called

sections.p: Your awareness stage is Google Local Services Ads (LSA), Google Ads search campaigns, or local SEO. We typically recommend a 60/40 split: 60% of budget toward local SEO (Google Business Profile optimization, local citations, location pages) because it's predictable at scale, and 40% toward paid ads for immediate traffic. A solar installer we worked with spent $1,200/month on Local Services Ads and generated 35-40 qualified calls monthly. That's about $30-35 per call, which converted to $12,000-15,000 in monthly revenue.

list: Google Business Profile: make sure your profile is fully filled out (photos, services, hours, posts). Update it every 2 weeks with new projects or seasonal services
Local citations: claim and optimize your NAP (name, address, phone) on Yelp, Angie's List, Home Advisor, and industry-specific directories
Location pages: if you serve multiple service areas, create individual pages for each (e.g., 'plumbing services in Denver', 'plumbing services in Aurora')
Google Ads search: bid on 'emergency [service] near me' and '[service] company [city]' keywords with a 30-40 character headline that includes your main differentiator

sections.p: Not every lead is worth your time. A roofing contractor we work with was taking every inquiry call, spending 45 minutes on consultations with people who had $500 budgets when their minimum project is $8,000. We implemented a simple qualification funnel: a landing page with a 90-second video of past work and a form asking for budget range, service area, and project timeline. This filtered 65% of tire-kickers before they ever got a call. The remaining 35% had a 58% conversion rate (vs. their previous 12%) because they were pre-qualified.

sections.quote: The biggest waste in service business marketing is talking to people who can't afford you. Qualify first, sell second.

sections.p: Your consideration stage should have: (1) a landing page with social proof (testimonials, project photos, certifications), (2) a simple form asking 3-4 questions (what's your project, when do you need it, do you have a budget?), and (3) an instant follow-up sequence via email or SMS. We've seen response rates jump 35-40% when contractors follow up with a qualified lead within 2 hours vs. next business day.

This is where most service businesses fail. They quote a price, the prospect disappears for 10 days, and suddenly you're chasing them. Instead, treat the quote as part of the conversation. A pest control company we worked with changed from emailing quotes to calling with the quote: 'Based on the size of your home and the treatment plan we discussed, this is going to be $850 for the initial service and $79/month for maintenance. Does that fit your budget?' This direct approach eliminated 3 weeks of back-and-forth and increased close rate from 22% to 41% because they addressed objections in real-time.

list: Quote in 24 hours, not 5 days. Speed builds momentum and shows professionalism
Include a clear next step: 'Here are our three available appointment times this week'
Use video proposals for jobs over $5,000. A 2-minute walk-through of the scope beats a written quote
Follow up once 48 hours after the initial quote if they don't respond. After that, one more email and move on

sections.h2: The Numbers You Need to Track

sections.p: If you're not measuring your funnel, you're flying blind. We recommend every service business track: (1) cost per lead from each channel (Google Ads, LSA, organic search, referrals), (2) qualification rate (% of leads that meet your criteria), (3) quote rate (% of qualified leads you actually quote), and (4) close rate (% of quotes that become customers). A plumbing company we work with had a 32% close rate but was only quoting 58% of their leads. By improving their sales process and adding video estimates, they increased quote rate to 78% and close rate to 44%—without buying more leads. That's a 34% revenue increase from the same marketing spend.

Build your funnel incrementally. Start with awareness (get found), add a simple qualification form (filter tire-kickers), then refine your quote process (close more of the right people). Most service businesses add 30-50% to annual revenue within 6 months just by plugging leaks in their existing funnel—before they ever increase ad spend.
